jokes.text: They're in new zealand dollars... it's a new zealand account
jokes.setup: Landlord complains about unpaid rent, guys confidently say they paid
jokes.punchline: Revelation they've been paying in wrong currency for two years
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment - their naive incompetence creates a massive problem they're oblivious to. Well-structured reveal.

dialogue.speaker: Landlord
dialogue.line: They're in new zealand dollars... it's a new zealand account

jokes.text: Is that American dollars or real dollars?
jokes.setup: Landlord states debt in American dollars
jokes.punchline: Bret treats NZ dollars as 'real' money, American as fake
jokes.explanation: Excellent character comedy - reveals Bret's provincial worldview. Highly quotable and economical.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Is that American dollars or real dollars?

jokes.text: Can we give you an I.O.U? In American dollars, not in new zealand dollars
jokes.setup: They owe massive debt and can't pay
jokes.punchline: Jemaine thinks currency matters for IOUs too
jokes.explanation: Good character consistency - they still don't understand the fundamental issue.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Can we give you an I.O.U? In American dollars, not in new zealand dollars

jokes.text: Never
jokes.setup: When will they have $7,727?
jokes.punchline: Brutally honest 'Never' delivered deadpan
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy and deadpan delivery. One word devastatingly honest response.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Never

jokes.text: Never.
jokes.setup: Asked when they'll have the money
jokes.punchline: Jemaine's brutally honest assessment delivered deadpan
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy - single word that completely deflates any hope. Classic Jemaine deadpan timing.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Never.

jokes.text: Can we choose which month?
jokes.setup: Landlord gives them one month to pay or leave
jokes.punchline: Bret thinks he can negotiate which month, missing the urgency
jokes.explanation: Brilliant misdirection showing Bret's complete misunderstanding of the situation's urgency.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Can we choose which month?

jokes.text: By whose authority? / The landlord. / Why? Just because we were paying with new zealand dollars instead of American dollars. / Okay, so he's a racist and now you're homeless
jokes.setup: Murray asks why they're evicted
jokes.punchline: Murray concludes landlord is racist for preferring American currency
jokes.explanation: Murray's logical leap from currency preference to racism shows his character's tendency to misinterpret situations.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: By whose authority?

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: The landlord.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Why?

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Just because we were paying with new zealand dollars instead of American dollars.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Okay, so he's a racist and now you're homeless

jokes.text: To stage your lives as an off-broadway musical
jokes.setup: They need practical solution to homelessness
jokes.punchline: Murray's solution is to turn their lives into a musical
jokes.explanation: Perfect Murray character moment - completely impractical but delivered with absolute conviction.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: To stage your lives as an off-broadway musical

jokes.text: Present.
jokes.setup: Murray calls their names in crisis meeting
jokes.punchline: They respond as if it's school attendance rather than emergency discussion
jokes.explanation: Perfectly captures their childlike approach to adult crises

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Present.

jokes.text: Our story is the story of two guys who start at the bottom and with a lot of hard work continue along the bottom and finally end up at the bottom
jokes.setup: Murray pitches rags-to-riches story structure
jokes.punchline: Jemaine accurately describes their actual trajectory
jokes.explanation: Excellent subversion of the typical success story structure with perfect economy and self-awareness.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Our story is the story of two guys who start at the bottom and with a lot of hard work continue along the bottom and finally end up at the bottom

jokes.text: Yeah, it's a rags-to-rags story
jokes.setup: Jemaine's description of their bottom-to-bottom journey
jokes.punchline: Bret coins 'rags-to-rags' story
jokes.explanation: Perfect callback that crystallizes the previous joke into a quotable phrase.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Yeah, it's a rags-to-rags story

jokes.text: Really? / Oh yeah, you say 'really?' here. I knew you'd say that
jokes.setup: Murray shows them the script he wrote about their lives
jokes.punchline: He predicted Bret's exact response in the script
jokes.explanation: Clever meta-joke about predictable dialogue and Murray's delusions of insight.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Really?

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Oh yeah, you say 'really?' here. I knew you'd say that

jokes.text: I've only got one bed and I grope people in my sleep. It's a condition
jokes.setup: Guys ask to stay at Murray's place
jokes.punchline: Murray matter-of-factly explains his sleep groping condition
jokes.explanation: Dark humor delivered with Murray's characteristic inappropriate oversharing.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: I've only got one bed and I grope people in my sleep. It's a condition

jokes.text: It's one of the reasons I had to leave the new zealand army
jokes.setup: Murray mentions his sleep groping condition
jokes.punchline: Reveals it caused military discharge
jokes.explanation: Good escalation that adds depth to the previous joke while maintaining Murray's oversharing character.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: It's one of the reasons I had to leave the new zealand army

jokes.text: That's actually better for the story, if you're homeless
jokes.setup: Guys decide not to stay at Murray's
jokes.punchline: Murray prioritizes his musical over their wellbeing
jokes.explanation: Perfect Murray moment - his artistic vision trumps basic human compassion.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: That's actually better for the story, if you're homeless

jokes.text: Your dad? / Yeah. I can't have you at my apartment. What if some crazy shit goes down?
jokes.setup: Dave mentions dangerous drug dealer roommate
jokes.punchline: Reveals the dangerous drug dealer is his father
jokes.explanation: Solid misdirection with good character-specific absurdity.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Your dad?

dialogue.speaker: Dave
dialogue.line: Yeah. I can't have you at my apartment. What if some crazy shit goes down?

jokes.text: She decapitated him, castrated him, defanged him. She removed his hands and feet
jokes.setup: Clerk describes a terrible murder in their only affordable room
jokes.punchline: Increasingly ridiculous list of body parts removed
jokes.explanation: Absurd escalation but feels somewhat generic without strong character specificity.

dialogue.speaker: Hotel clerk
dialogue.line: She decapitated him, castrated him, defanged him. She removed his hands and feet

jokes.text: Oh, that's today's date. / Weird. / Yeah, it's today's date. It happened earlier today
jokes.setup: Murder happened on November 1st
jokes.punchline: Casual revelation it happened earlier the same day
jokes.explanation: Great timing and escalation that makes the absurd situation even more absurd.

dialogue.line: Oh, that's today's date.
Weird.
Yeah, it's today's date. It happened earlier today

jokes.text: There will be no bringing girls back to the house. I know how you guys are
jokes.setup: Mel lays down house rules
jokes.punchline: Assumes Bret and Jemaine are successful with women
jokes.explanation: Good irony given their complete lack of success with women throughout the series.

dialogue.speaker: Mel
dialogue.line: There will be no bringing girls back to the house. I know how you guys are

jokes.text: Yeah, maybe I'll bring some girls home too, just find some real authentic sluts and just turn this house into a whorehome
jokes.setup: Doug suggests boys should be allowed to bring girls over
jokes.punchline: Mel escalates to absurd extreme of turning house into brothel
jokes.explanation: Characteristic Mel overreaction that reveals her controlling and dramatic nature.

dialogue.speaker: Mel
dialogue.line: Yeah, maybe I'll bring some girls home too, just find some real authentic sluts and just turn this house into a whorehome

jokes.text: You must not leave the house. / When? / What, never? / Well, not after 11:30 P.M. And preferably never
jokes.setup: Mel establishing house rules about going out
jokes.punchline: Escalation from curfew to preferably never leaving
jokes.explanation: Good escalation showing Mel's controlling nature and desire to keep them captive.

dialogue.speaker: Mel
dialogue.line: You must not leave the house.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: When?

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: What, never?

dialogue.speaker: Mel
dialogue.line: Well, not after 11:30 P.M. And preferably never

jokes.text: That is such a womanly instrument. Have you ever heard of a man playing a harp? / My dad plays the harp
jokes.setup: Mel declares harp a womanly instrument, says no men play it
jokes.punchline: Bret immediately contradicts with personal example
jokes.explanation: Clean setup-punchline with perfect timing and economy.

dialogue.speaker: Mel
dialogue.line: That is such a womanly instrument. Have you ever heard of a man playing a harp?

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: My dad plays the harp

jokes.text: I sometimes go to the toilet in the middle of the night. / Mm-hmm. I know
jokes.setup: Jemaine asks Mel not to lock the bedroom door
jokes.punchline: Mel's creepy acknowledgment that she knows his bathroom habits
jokes.explanation: Perfectly creepy Mel moment that adds to the uncomfortable living situation.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: I sometimes go to the toilet in the middle of the night.

dialogue.speaker: Mel
dialogue.line: Mm-hmm. I know

jokes.text: Petrov, Yelyena and me / lost but happy at sea
jokes.setup: Dream sequence begins with romantic sailing scenario
jokes.punchline: Setup for cannibalism story disguised as pleasant sailing
jokes.explanation: Excellent misdirection setting up what appears to be a romantic song before turning dark.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Petrov, Yelyena and me / lost but happy at sea

jokes.text: So I said, 'do you know any Rolling Stones?' It was a hilarious moment in a very bleak bleak time of my life.
jokes.setup: About to be eaten by cannibals
jokes.punchline: Bret's completely inappropriate assessment of the moment as 'hilarious'
jokes.explanation: Perfect deadpan misreading of a horrific situation - classic Bret character beat

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: So I said, 'do you know any Rolling Stones?' It was a hilarious moment in a very bleak bleak time of my life.

jokes.text: How come it looks so much like my arm which has been hacked off at the elbow last night? / Oh, it's an arm fish
jokes.setup: Jemaine questions Petrov about eating what looks like his arm
jokes.punchline: Petrov's absurd excuse that it's an 'arm fish'
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly absurd excuse delivered deadpan, 'arm fish' is perfectly ridiculous and quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: How come it looks so much like my arm which has been hacked off at the elbow last night?

dialogue.speaker: Petrov
dialogue.line: Oh, it's an arm fish

jokes.text: This seems very similar to 'Star Wars.' / I've mixed you up with... Luke Skywalker
jokes.setup: Murray's musical script about their lives
jokes.punchline: Murray accidentally wrote Star Wars instead of their story
jokes.explanation: Good meta-joke about derivative writing and Murray's incompetence as a writer.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: This seems very similar to 'Star Wars.'

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: I've mixed you up with... Luke Skywalker

jokes.text: Doug and I have something very important to talk to you about. We have decided to spend some time apart
jokes.setup: Mel and Doug need to talk to the boys about something important
jokes.punchline: Treating the guys like children in a divorce scenario
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection that frames the situation like a parental divorce announcement.

dialogue.speaker: Mel
dialogue.line: Doug and I have something very important to talk to you about. We have decided to spend some time apart

jokes.text: This isn't about you. / Well, actually, Mel, it is about them
jokes.setup: Mel reassures the boys the breakup isn't about them
jokes.punchline: Doug immediately contradicts, revealing it IS about them
jokes.explanation: Perfect subversion of the typical divorce speech with immediate contradiction.

dialogue.speaker: Mel
dialogue.line: This isn't about you.

dialogue.speaker: Doug
dialogue.line: Well, actually, Mel, it is about them

jokes.text: You're so young. / I'm 32
jokes.setup: Mel patronizes Bret about being too young to understand
jokes.punchline: Bret's deadpan correction that he's 32
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy and deadpan delivery highlighting the absurdity of treating a 32-year-old like a child.

dialogue.speaker: Mel
dialogue.line: You're so young.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: I'm 32

jokes.text: All the professional actors write their lines down on each other's clothing
jokes.setup: Murray explains why actors need to write lines on clothes
jokes.punchline: Completely false claim about professional acting presented as fact
jokes.explanation: Perfect Murray moment - completely wrong information delivered with absolute authority.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: All the professional actors write their lines down on each other's clothing

jokes.text: The longer the play, the bigger the shirt. You'll always notice that... big costumes. Why do they always go like this? There's lines covered in all the clothing
jokes.setup: Murray's theory about writing lines on clothing
jokes.punchline: Elaborate false explanation for why period costumes are large
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of the clothing-lines joke with Murray's characteristic false confidence.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: The longer the play, the bigger the shirt. You'll always notice that... big costumes. Why do they always go like this? There's lines covered in all the clothing

jokes.text: I've noticed your acting tends to be a bit boring. It's okay for the first minute, then I kind of drift off
jokes.setup: Murray giving acting direction to Jemaine
jokes.punchline: Brutally honest criticism delivered matter-of-factly
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Murray's tactless but honest management style.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: I've noticed your acting tends to be a bit boring. It's okay for the first minute, then I kind of drift off

jokes.text: Yesterday's rehearsal you got yourself worked up, you stopped breathing and you fainted. No, I didn't. You were on the ground there for a while, about five minutes.
jokes.setup: Murray giving breathing advice to Bret
jokes.punchline: Bret denied fainting despite being unconscious for five minutes
jokes.explanation: Bret's denial of obvious physical reality

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Yesterday's rehearsal you got yourself worked up, you stopped breathing and you fainted.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: No, I didn't.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: You were on the ground there for a while, about five minutes.

jokes.text: Now scram, you filthy illegal immigrants
jokes.setup: Musical recreation of their eviction scene
jokes.punchline: Over-the-top villain dialogue in the musical version
jokes.explanation: Callback to earlier scene but with theatrical exaggeration typical of bad musicals.

dialogue.speaker: Landlord
dialogue.line: Now scram, you filthy illegal immigrants

jokes.text: Whoo! Look at the harp player! / It is manly
jokes.setup: Doug playing harp in the musical
jokes.punchline: Callback to earlier argument about harp being unmanly
jokes.explanation: Good callback that resolves the earlier harp gender debate.

dialogue.speaker: Audience member
dialogue.line: Whoo! Look at the harp player!

dialogue.speaker: Mel
dialogue.line: It is manly

jokes.text: Well, it depends how you define 'hit.' / Did people like it? / No, I don't think it was that sort of hit
jokes.setup: Murray asked if the musical was a hit
jokes.punchline: Murray implies it was a different kind of 'hit' (failure)
jokes.explanation: Clever wordplay with perfect deadpan delivery about their inevitable failure.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Well, it depends how you define 'hit.'

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Did people like it?

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: No, I don't think it was that sort of hit

jokes.text: Unfortunately the play drew a lot of attention to your illegal immigrant status
jokes.setup: Murray invited embassy officials to see the show
jokes.punchline: Their musical about being illegal immigrants exposed them to authorities
jokes.explanation: Excellent irony - Murray's solution creates an even bigger problem through spectacular oversight.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Unfortunately the play drew a lot of attention to your illegal immigrant status

jokes.text: Good shepherding today, guys! / Thanks, Murray!
jokes.setup: They're back to shepherding in New Zealand
jokes.punchline: Circular ending - they're exactly where they started
jokes.explanation: Perfect circular ending that fulfills the 'rags-to-rags' prediction from earlier.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Good shepherding today, guys!

dialogue.speaker: Bret or Jemaine
dialogue.line: Thanks, Murray!
